}} '''Michael Forshaw''' (born 5 January 1970) is an English [[rugby union]] coach, and former professional [[rugby league]] and rugby union player. He is currently the defence coach for the [[Leicester Tigers]]. Forshaw began his playing career with [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in 1987 and went on to play for [[Wakefield Trinity]], [[Bradford Bulls]] and [[Warrington Wolves]]. He played 14 times for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] and 3 for [[England national rugby league team|England]]. ==Background== Forshaw was born on 5 January 1970 in [[Wigan]], Lancashire. ==Playing career== ===Rugby league=== ===Club=== Forshaw started his career with [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. He was a largely a fringe player during his time at the club, but did make an appearance from the interchange bench in Wigan's [[1991 World Club Challenge]] victory against the visiting [[Penrith Panthers]]. He joined [[Wakefield Trinity]] in September 1993.{{cite news |title=Trinity sign Forshaw |work=[[The Times]] |date=9 September 1993 |location=London |page=40}} After a brief spell playing [[rugby union]] for [[Saracens F.C.]], he returned to rugby league in April 1997, joining [[Bradford Bulls]].{{cite web |title=Goodway makes way at Oldham |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/goodway-makes-way-at-oldham-1268838.html |website=The Independent |access-date=29 March 2025 |date=22 April 1997}} Despite joining the club mid-season, he impressed enough to be named in the 1997 [[Super League Dream Team]].{{cite news |last = Hadfield |first = Dave |title = Forshaw's dreams come true |work = [[The Independent]] |location=London |date = 17 September 1997 |url = https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-forshaws-dreams-come-true-1239656.html |accessdate = 24 April 2010}} Forshaw played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] from the interchange bench in the [[1999 Super League Grand Final]] which was lost to [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. Forshaw played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] as a {{rlp|lf}} in their [[2001 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Wigan Warriors]]. As [[Super League VI]] champions, the Bradford Bulls played against the [[2001 NRL season|2001 NRL]] Premiers, the [[Newcastle Knights]] in the [[2002 World Club Challenge]]. Forshaw played as a {{rlp|lf}} in Bradford's victory.{{cite web |title=Bulls clinch World Club Challenge |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/photo_galleries/1796785.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=1 April 2025 |date=1 February 2002}} Forshaw played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] as a {{rlp|lf}} in their [[2002 Super League Grand Final]] loss against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. Forshaw played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] as a {{rlp|lf}} in their [[2003 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Wigan Warriors]]. He signed a one-year contract with the [[Warrington Wolves]] for the 2004 season,{{cite web |title=Wolves land Forshaw |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/warrington/3205855.stm |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=8 May 2023 |date=23 October 2003}} where he finished his playing career. ===International=== Forshaw was named in the [[England national rugby league team|England]] squad for the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]],{{cite web |title=World Cup squad looks Bullish |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/930267.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=1 April 2025 |date=18 September 2000}} and won [[Cap (sport)|cap]]s against Australia, Ireland, and New Zealand. In the 1997 post-season, Forshaw was selected to play for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] in two matches of the [[Super League Test series]] against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]]. Forshaw won [[Cap (sport)|cap]]s for Great Britain while at Bradford Bulls in 1997 against [[Super League (Australia)|Australia (ASL)]] (2 matches) ([[Rugby league positions#Interchange|interchange]]/[[Substitution (sport)|substitute]]), in 1998 against New Zealand ([[Rugby league positions#Interchange|interchange]]/[[Substitution (sport)|substitute]]), in 1999 against New Zealand ([[Rugby league positions#Interchange|interchange]]/[[Substitution (sport)|substitute]]), in 2001 against France ([[Rugby league positions#Interchange|interchange]]/[[Substitution (sport)|substitute]]), and Australia (3 matches), in 2002 against New Zealand (3 matches), and in 2003 against Australia (2 matches), and Australia ([[Rugby league positions#Interchange|interchange]]/[[Substitution (sport)|substitute]]). ==Coaching career== ===Rugby league=== In 2004, Forshaw was appointed in a part-time role by the [[Rugby Football League]] as a performance lifestyle advisor.{{cite web |title=Forshaw to be player guru |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2004/jun/16/rugbyleague.warrington |website=The Guardian |access-date=1 April 2025 |date=16 June 2004}} After ending his playing career, he rejoined [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] in May 2005 as a strength and conditioning coach.{{cite web |title=Forshaw is handed key Wolves role |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/warrington/4541141.stm |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=8 May 2023 |date=12 May 2005}} In November 2006, he returned to his boyhood club and teamed up with his old coach [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] when he was appointed to a similar coaching role at [[Wigan Warriors]].{{cite web |title=Forshaw takes Wigan coaching role |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/6106444.stm |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=8 May 2023 |date=4 November 2006}} He studied a BSc part-time at [[UCLAN]] following Wigan's sponsorship by UCLAN.{{citation needed|date=July 2020}} ===Rugby union=== In 2010, Forshaw joined [[Connacht Rugby|Connacht]] in the [[Pro14|Celtic League]] as defence coach.{{cite web |url=http://www.connachtrugby.ie/2010062282238/connacht-appoint-defence-coach |title=Connacht appoint Defence Coach | Connacht Rugby Official Website |accessdate=2010-06-29 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20101229231102/http://www.connachtrugby.ie/2010062282238/connacht-appoint-defence-coach |archivedate=29 December 2010}} In 2013, he joined the [[Sale Sharks]] in the same role.{{cite web|url=http://www.salesharks.com/news/1899.php#.Vj0U67fhDIU|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304192836/http://www.salesharks.com/news/1899.php#.Vj0U67fhDIU|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 March 2016|title=Add Reference Name|publisher=salesharks.com|date=31 December 2013|accessdate=1 January 2014}} Forshaw follows a '3D' approach to the contact area of defence: discipline, detail and decision-making, with a particular focus when within 50m of his team's own try line.{{Cite web|url=https://www.therugbypaper.co.uk/featured-post/10539/mike-forshaw-talks-tough-to-danny-cipriani-and-co/|title=Mike Forshaw talks tough to Danny Cipriani and Co|date=9 August 2013}} In July 2020 Forshaw extended his contract with Sale until the end of the 2022 season.{{Cite news |title=Sale coaching duo sign new contracts |language=en-GB |publisher=BBC Sport |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-union/53406397 |access-date=2023-01-12}} In 2023, after a decade at Sale, Forshaw was appointed as defence coach for the [[Wales national rugby union team|Wales national team]] for the [[2023 Six Nations Championship]].{{Cite web |date=2023-01-12 |title=Forshaw and King join Wales senior men's coaching team – Welsh Rugby Union |url=https://www.wru.wales/2023/01/forshaw-and-king-join-wales-senior-mens-coaching-team/ |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=Welsh Rugby Union {{!}} Wales & Regions |language=en-GB}} ==References==
